data.title: Ideaya Biosciences: Value Beyond Darovasertib With ADC Program IDE849
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Ideaya Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:IDYA) it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Ideaya Biosciences: Potential Registration-Enabling Darovasertib Study Coming." With respect to this particular article, I noted that the company was advancing its lead small molecule inhibitor of PKC darovasertib for the treatment of patients with metastatic uveal melanoma [MUM] in an ongoing phase 2/3 registration-enabling trial. In particular, this drug is being explored in this study for the treatment of these patients in combination with crizotinib. A median progression-free survival [mPFS] data readout for this registration-enabling study is still expected to happen by the end of 2025.

Not only that, but the company has even been able to make progress with the FDA in a Type D meeting, whereby it will be able to initiate a phase 3 registration-enabling study of darovasertib as a neoadjuvant for the treatment of patients with Uveal Melanoma [UM]. Such a late-stage study for these particular patients is expected to initiate in the 1st half of 2025. This time around, though, I believe that there is a chance for this company to create additional shareholder value with another candidate from its pipeline, which is known as IDE849. The company obtained worldwide license agreement from Hengrui Pharma to develop it. That is, it can develop it across the entire globe for any territory besides that of the Greater China region. What makes this candidate ideal is that it is an antibody-drug conjugate [ADC] that targets DLL3 expressing solid tumors.

The two types of solid tumors in question being targeted are small cell lung cancer [SCLC] and neuroendocrine tumors [NETs]. Why do I believe that this is another possible program to generate shareholder value? That's because there are two catalysts for investors to look forward to this year. The first of which is that its partner Hengrui Pharma is going to release data from a phase 1 study in China using IDE849 for the treatment of patients with SCLC at a medical conference in Q3 of 2025. The second catalyst of which is that the company intends to initiate an early-stage study of IDE849 in combination with PARG inhibitor IDE161 for the treatment of patients with soldi tumors in the 2nd half of 2025.

Two Catalysts In 2025 For ADC Candidate Could Launch Another Solid Program Into The Mix

As I noted above, Ideaya Biosciences is currently in the process of developing its lead drug candidate darovasertib in combination with crizotinib for the treatment of patients with uveal melanoma. This is being done in a phase 2/3 registrational trial, whereby a total of more than 300 patients have been enrolled as of May 5th of 2025. Plus, there is going to be the initiation of a a phase 3 registration-enabling study of darovasertib as a neoadjuvant for the treatment of patients with uveal melanoma. However, I believe that it is important to go over its development of its antibody-drug conjugate [ADC] program that it had been able to license from its China partner Hengrui Pharma.

The reason why I believe there is value here is because the mechanism of action [MOA] of ADCs is proven through a multitude of clinical trials. This doesn't mean that this candidate is going to end up being successful with all of the studies it is being evaluated in, but it does mean that it has a greater chance of being successful.

The end game goal of this company is to use ADC IDE849 for the treatment of patients with two types of solid tumors, which again are Small Cell Lung Cancer [SCLC] and Neuroendocrine Tumors [NETs]. The company was able to receive IND clearance from the U.S. FDA to begin a phase 1 study for the treatment of patients with SCLC. However, this program is currently also being done by its partner Hengrui Pharma. Specifically, it is running a phase 1 study in China using IDE849 to treat patients with SCLC.

Before going over this study from its partner, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, it is first important to note what this type of cancer is and what the possible market opportunity it could end up with. While not a necessary focus at first, it might also be noteworthy to speak about neuroendocrine tumors as well.

Lung Cancer is, as the name suggests, the growth of malignant cells on the lung that forms a solid tumor. In particular, the growth of such cancer tends to occur in the airways or air sacs [alveoli] of the lungs. In this type of cancer, a patient is not only affected in terms of disease itself, but the fact that it has an impact on breathing. That's because the functions of the lungs is to allow a person to breathe in an effective manner. While most cancers tend to occur randomly due to mutated cells, there is something that actually causes an increase in lung cancer, which is smoking. Those who smoke are at greater risk of getting this type of cancer.

It is important to highlight that there are two types of lung cancer that a person could end up having:

  • Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer [NSCLC]
  • Small-Cell Lung Cancer [SCLC].

These two types occur and they are coined as such depending upon how they look under a microscope for starters. Not only that, but each one has different characteristics that sets them apart. In terms of SCLC, it is not the more common type. Why do I state that? That's because it only accounts for between 10% to 15% of all lung cancer cases. On the other hand, NSCLC accounts for between 80% to 85% of all cases.

While that is true, SCLC can be more devastating. Not only does it grow/spread in a more rapid manner, but it is also far more difficult to treat than that of NSCLC. In the 7 major markets the global small cell lung cancer market is expected to grow to $23 billion by 2035. Despite the emphasis of this company's focus of only going after SCLC patients who have DLL3 expression, this would still be a good market opportunity for it regardless. The reason why this is the cases is because it is said that about 85% of SCLC tumors express DLL3. Not only is the development of IDE849 ideal because of this finding, but DLL3 is minimally expressed in healthy tissue. If you check out the company's May 2025 Corporate Presentation [slide #20], you will see the percentage of SCLC patients it could go after along with many other solid tumor types.

The thing is that this company's partner Hengrui Pharma is already running an open-label, multicenter phase 1 study evaluating the use of IDE849 [SHR-4849] for the treatment of patients with solid tumors. Since it is an early-stage trial, the primary outcome measures have been established to be that in terms of safety. These include items such as: Dose-limiting toxicities [DLTs], maximum tolerated dose [MTD], recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D] and adverse events/serious adverse events [AEs/SAEs]. There are several secondary efficacy outcome measures that are to be evaluated, but with the most notable ones in my opinion being Objective Response Rate [ORR] and Overall Survival [OS]. The reason why I brought up this study is because its partner in China, Hengrui Pharma, has been running it for an extended period of time. Having said that, it expects to present safety/efficacy data from over 40 SCLC patients from this trial in Q3 of 2025 at a medical conference in Q3 of 2025.

Further Expansion Of IDE849 Possible In Combination Regimen

Not only did Ideaya Biosciences obtain IND clearance from the FDA to begin a phase 1 study in using IDE849 for the treatment of patients with advanced solid tumors, but there might actually be an expansion opportunity. In essence, there is the potential for another shot on goal in the development of this particular DLL3-targeting ADC. This would be in terms of combining IDE849 together with another clinical drug from its pipeline known as IDE161.

The thing is that IDE849 takes care of targeting DLL3 on the surface of cancer cells to destroy them, but why does this company believe that adding IDE161 into the mix is a good idea? it could end up allowing such advanced solid tumor patients to achieve a better response rate. What happens with IDE161 is that it is a PARG inhibitor that is responsible for disrupting the process of DNA repair of cancer cells. The truth is that this process of disrupting the process of DNA damage repair is increased in cancer where DNA repair is already deficient such as in cancers with specific biomarkers like BRCA, DLL3 and other types of expressing solid tumors.

The goal for Ideaya here is to initiate a study that is to evaluate IDE849 in combination with IDE161 for the treatment of patients with solid tumors. The company believes that it could initiate such a particular combination study of ID849 in the 2nd half of 2025.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Ideaya Biosciences had cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $1.05 billion as of March 31st of 2025. The truth is that it has plenty of cash on hand, thus I don't foresee any risk of dilution happening. Especially, when you consider the guidance it had given as part of tits 10-K SEC filing. It stated that it believes it has enough of a cash runway to fund its operations into 2029. The reason for the cash on hand was because of an underwritten public offering that was completed on July 11th of 2024.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Ideaya Biosciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the release of PFS data from the ongoing phase 2/3 registration-enabling study of darovasertib + crizotinib combination for the treatment of patients with 1st-line HLA-A2-negative metastatic uveal melanoma [MUM]. Such PFS data is expected by year-end and there is no assurance that this endpoint is going to be met in a statistically significant manner upon release. Nor, that the company will be able to file for Accelerated Approval of this darovasertib combination for the treatment of these specific MUM patients thereafter.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of an ongoing phase 2 study of darovasertib in combination with either crizotinib or binimetib for the treatment of 40 1st-line MUM patients. That's because the goal of this company is to release overall-survival [OS] data from this mid-stage trial at a medical conference in the 2nd half of 2025. It is important to note that this particular trial is not only looking at 1st-line HLA-A2-negative MUM patients, but also those who are HLA-A2-positive as well under this setting. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be released from this study is going to be positive for starters. Nor, that the OS data released from this phase 2 study is going to be perceived in a positive manner by the stock market.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the development of antibody-drug conjugate [ADC] IDE849 for the treatment of patients with SCLC and NETs. Specifically, its partner Hengrui Pharma is evaluating the use of this therapy for the treatment of 40 patients with SCLC in a phase 1 dose-escalation and multiple expansion-dose study. Data from this specific early-stage trial is going to be presented at a medical conference in Q3 of 2025. The risk here is that upon release of data from its collaboration partner in China, there is no assurance that the final data will end up being positive. Nor, that the data is ultimately going to be good enough to warrant further investigation into a phase 2 clinical study.

Conclusion

Ideaya Biosciences has done well to advance its lead development program of darovasertib for the treatment of patients with metastatic uveal melanoma [MUM]. Specifically, it is gearing up to release mPFS data from its ongoing phase 2/3 registration-enabling study using darovasertib + crizotinib to target 1st-line HLA-A2-negative MUM patients by the end of 2025. This is only the beginning in terms of what is possible for this biotech in being able to use its first-in-class PKC inhibitor drug to target these patients. It has also expanded this program to include HLA-A2-positive 1st-line MUM patients and the use of this drug as a neoadjuvant therapy for these primary UM patients as well.

With the release of data from a phase 1 study from its partner Hengrui using ADC IDE849 for the treatment of patients with SCLC at a medical conference in China in Q3 of 2025, plus the continued development of its lead darovasertib program targeting UM patients, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: Recursion Pharmaceuticals: High-Risk Play Getting Riskier By The Quarter
data.description:
Introduction

Recursion Pharmaceuticals’ stock (NASDAQ:RXRX) has slid 57% since my last look in February. Despite the company leveraging artificial intelligence in drug discovery, I was “unconvinced” in its lead drug, REC-994, that targets cerebral cavernous malformation or CCM. Moreover, I noted that other clinical candidates, like REC-3964 for the prevention of rCDI, faced crowded competition and limited differentiation. Despite the recent Exscientia merger, which boosted its cash holdings, I noted that its monthly cash burn rate of $20 million “demands clear clinical success.” I ended with a Hold rating, leaning slightly bearish.

There have been some developments, and Recursion has reported its first quarter earnings since February, so I wanted to take another look at the stock.

Recent Developments

Recursion’s first quarter results reveal some worrying trends.

In the clinic, Recursion has decided to part ways with REC-994 in CCM, which is not surprising given my past write-ups. Notably, this was Recursion’s lead asset and was supposed to showcase the company’s AI edge in drug discovery, which should, theoretically, lead to better clinical outcomes.

Elsewhere, REC-2282 for NF2, too, will bite the dust. The drug conferred “limited overall tumor shrinkage and clinical activity across all arms.” In other words, it simply did not work. This was another major focus of Recursion.

Lastly, REC-3964 for rCDI (C. difficile) underwhelmed. Moreover, Recursion noted that “evolved treatment options result in low recurrence rates; thus limiting unmet need.” Instead of disposing of it, Recursion hopes to out-license the asset. The keyword is “hope.” I doubt that Recursion will extract too much value from REC-3964, given its limited differentiation from available treatments.

So, that’s three significant setbacks for Recursion in a short time. Its pipeline has gone from this:

To this:

I almost forgot to mention a “strategic pause” for REC-4539.

To put things bluntly, these are concerning updates for its rare disease pipeline.

Recursion's Oncology Pivot: Challenges and Opportunities

So, the attention now turns to its oncology assets. Recursion is targeting “advanced solid tumors,” “B-cell malignancies,” and “breast cancer.” These are all heavily contested fields with multiple approved therapies. What was, at least, compelling about a condition like CVM was that there were no approved treatments. If Recursion successfully markets a product for CVM, it would have had a lot of pricing power and market share, albeit for a rare condition. When you target a condition like breast cancer, simply getting a product to the “finish line” is not enough.

REC-7735 is being positioned as a “best-in-class” (BIC) breast cancer drug. It is “designed to enhance therapeutic index.” Current PI3K pathway inhibitors have proven effective in some types of breast cancer, but their use is limited by toxicities like hyperglycemia (high blood sugar), rash, and diarrhea. REC-7735 supposedly targets the H1047R mutant form of PI3K, sparing the wild-type form and avoiding its associated toxicities (as evidenced by data in mice). This specific mutation is found in approximately 15% of HR+/HER2- breast cancers. There are a few approved drugs that exploit the PI3K pathway already, like Novartis’ (NVS) Piqray, AstraZeneca’s (AZN) Truqap, and Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) Itovebi. The last drug appears to be a major improvement over past PI3K inhibitors, with a grade 3-4 hyperglycemia rate of 5.6% compared to ~37% in Piqray. Itovebi was approved last October for HR+/HER2- breast cancer, and Roche believes it can generate over $2 billion at its peak. Alongside REC-7735, there are a few other next-generation mutant-selective inhibitors in development (e.g., OKI-219, LY4045004, and STX-478). The last drug inspired Eli Lilly to acquire Scorpion Therapeutics for $2.5 billion earlier this year. STX-478 is further along in development. Phase 1 data revealed responses across multiple PI3K-mutated advanced solid tumors without any grade 3 occurrences of hyperglycemia, diarrhea, and rash (n=43).

Financial Health

Financially, while Recursion does recognize revenue from collaborations (e.g., Roche, Merck) and via its drug discovery engine, the Recursion Operating System (OS), its cash burn accelerated. In the first quarter of 2025, Recursion utilized $132 million in operating activities, implying a monthly cash burn of over $40 million. A one-time transaction of $16 million did contribute to the increase, but it was largely driven by higher R&D and G&A costs post-merger. Recursion’s expected cash burn in 2025 is “equal to or less than $450 million.” Thanks to $500.5 million in cash and cash equivalents, Recursion believes its cash runway extends into mid-2027. Given that Recursion’s clinical assets are all in early stages of development, the company may have to resort to shareholder-unfriendly solutions to raise capital.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, a lot has changed at Recursion, and almost all of it is bad. As I alluded to in my prior analysis, Recursion needs a clinical win more than ever to justify its exuberant R&D investments. Just the opposite has happened. Granted, there’s some optionality. Revenue from Recursion OS and big pharma collaborations does help offset some of Recursion’s costs. Moreover, REC-7735 is an interesting asset, but it is also early, and the PI3K inhibitor landscape is rapidly evolving. Besides, Recursion’s market capitalization is nearing $2 billion despite recent pipeline setbacks, high cash burn, and early-stage clinical candidates. In this vein, it’s not like REC-775 is being valued at zero. It could be easily argued that Recursion is overvalued, and this might account for the high short interest. RXRX remains a Hold (“market underperform”), but this is only in the context of a barbell portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: OTC Markets Group: Higher Trading Volumes And Market Data Price Increases
data.description:
Introduction

OTC Markets (OTCQX:OTCM) recently reported its Q1’25 results on May 7. During the quarter, elevated trading volume drove positive results, and market data price increases drove the way for top-line growth. Since the last time I reviewed the company almost a year ago, OTC Markets’ stock has fallen about 14% while the S&P 500 has returned the same figure in the opposite direction. In my view, with a stable capital structure and progress toward expanding its product suite to drive growth, I’m really encouraged by OTC Markets’ progress in Q1, and I find the valuation at 14.4x EV/EBITDA attractive at current prices.

Q1’25 Overview

In Q1’25, OTC Markets reported a mixed quarter with revenues growing 10% to $30.4 million, driven primarily by a 22% surge in OTC Link revenues and a 15% rise in market data licensing revenues. While the revenue figure was a $2.3 million beat compared to sell-side estimates, the company missed on the bottom line. As shown below, the company’s total operating expenses increased by 6%, in line with revenue, but lower other income and a higher tax rate meant that net income was flat year over year at $0.50, which was $0.03 below consensus.

Strong revenue trends in OTC Link and Market Data Licensing

Starting with revenue, I’d say that OTC Link’s performance is looking great. OTC Link revenue increased 22% compared to last year, stemming from a 46% increase in revenue from OTC Link ECN and OTC Link NQB, driven by a higher volume of shares traded on those platforms. OTC Link averaged about 56,000 daily trades in Q1’25 compared to approximately 34,000 during Q1’24. While OTC Link transaction-derived revenue has trended favorably in recent quarters, it’s really hard to gauge whether this can continue since trading volumes remain unpredictable. Given the volatility we’ve seen in markets lately, Q2 might be a good quarter for the segment, but it's uncertain how it will fare going forward.

In OTC’s largest revenue segment, Market Data Licensing revenue was 15% in Q1’25 compared to last year, largely due to an increase in redistributor-based revenue from professional user licenses as a result of price increases that went into effect at the beginning of 2025. On the earnings call, management noted that they’re continuing to invest in talent and infrastructure to manage growth and product expansion, a trend I expect to continue. Compensation and benefit expenses led a 6% increase in operating expenses in Q1’25, and operating income rose 9% due to healthy revenue growth, while operating margin was roughly flat at 25%. Despite the company’s effective tax rate increasing nearly 600 basis points due to a lower excess tax benefit on stock-based compensation in Q1'25, EPS rose to $0.50 from $0.49 in Q1'24.

In the Market Data Licensing business, one of the takeaways I found interesting from the quarter was that while the number of professional users subscribing to the company’s data products decreased 1% to 27,711, the company was able to extract bottom-line growth through material price increases introduced at the beginning of the year. These price increases of 17% to more than 40% for certain professional users drove a 20% year-over-year increase in revenue from professional users.

Going forward, I’d expect a similar benefit in the remainder of 2025, especially as the company develops and enhances the relevance of data products for subscribers. OTC Link continues to experience elevated trading volume on its platforms, with the total dollar volume traded in OTCQX, OTCQB, and Pink securities up 57% in Q1’25 to $183 billion.

Lastly, in the Corporate Services segment, OTC Markets continues to face macroeconomic headwinds weighing on subscriber growth, with revenue 1% lower in the quarter. Lower results from OTCQB led the decline, with 1,051 companies on OTCQB compared to 1,115 in Q1’25. OTCQB saw a slightly lower retention rate at the start of the year, partially offset by higher sales and a lower number of non-renewals and compliance downgrades. OTCQX saw a 1% increase in revenue due to price increases that offset a lower number of company subscribers. Despite near-term economic challenges, I think the value proposition to listing on OTCM market platforms continues to grow, specifically for international companies aiming to access U.S. capital markets. On the conference call post-quarter, management commented that the new market tier set to launch in July, OTCID, has seen early interest and has influenced a positive trend in Corporate Services sales.

Balance sheet and capital allocation

When looking at the company’s balance sheet, I think OTC looks to be in good shape. On adjusted EBITDA of $9.8 million in the quarter (roughly $40 million annualized), the company allocates the bulk of their free cash flow towards dividends and buybacks. During the quarter, $2.2 million went towards dividends and $2.9 million of shares were repurchased.

Given that the company has virtually no debt, I expect capital returns to continue to improve as the company’s top and bottom line continue to grow. While an $0.18 quarterly dividend may seem small, the company often pays special dividends at year-end, a policy that it’s continued for several years—on top of an already expanding regular dividend. Assuming the company pays four $0.18 dividends and a $1.50 special dividend for the year, the company’s payout ratio is roughly 24%. That signals to me that the company has ample room to raise the dividend every year, even if a few years are sluggish in terms of growth.

Risks to consider

I think a business like OTC Markets is pretty recession-resilient; it’s got all the characteristics of a durable business that can survive both good times and bad. After all, investors will continue to trade in both up markets and down markets, and data and information services have recurring revenue aspects that make them attractive.

However, that doesn’t mean that the company is without risk. First, the volatility of trading volumes has a level of uncertainty that investors should be aware of as it relates to OTC Link’s revenue stream, which accounted for a 22% increase compared to last year. Don’t count on this growth. A sharp drop in market activity, such as those driven by macroeconomic shifts or reduced retail investor participation, could erode transaction-based revenues, squeezing margins. But even though volumes are inherently unpredictable, I’d expect them to increase in general over the long run.

The other risk would be rising OPEX. Operating expenses grew 6% compared to last year, and given that they increased just barely below revenue, I worry that continued cost escalation could further compress operating margins, which already dipped to 24.7% from 25% last year. This tells me that disciplined cost management will be critical to sustaining the company’s financial health, especially if revenue growth slows. While some of this could be tied to investments in overnight trading or OTCID, it’s something that I think investors should be watching in the quarters ahead.

Valuation

OTC Markets’ stock is undercovered by the sell-side, with no Wall Street analysts covering the stock. On Seeking Alpha, views have been neutral to bullish by authors who cover the stock. To me, I see good value in the share price today. When looking at the company’s valuation of 14.4x EV/EBITDA and 21.3x P/E, I think this is relatively underpriced versus peers who trade at 21.0x EV/EBITDA and 36.9x P/E (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Given OTC Markets is a debt-free company with a high margin, recurring revenue business, I think it deserves to trade at least in line with the peer group. At 14.4x EV/EBITDA, the stock trades below where it was the last time I valued it last year when it traded for 17.2x EV/EBITDA. For a company, that can likely grow its EPS in the high-single digits to low double digits, I find the valuation to be compelling.

Conclusion

All in all, I'm still bullish on OTC Markets, as I'm encouraged by the company’s ability to navigate a tougher market environment while advancing strategic initiatives like OTCID and overnight trading. At 14.4x EV/EBITDA, investors have yet to award this company a respectable multiple, which it deserves because of its debt-free cap stack and commitment to capital returns to shareholders through dividends and buybacks. To me, the early traction in MOON ATS and OTC Overnight, combined with the upcoming OTCID launch, positions OTC Markets to capture long-term growth in global trading and transparency-driven markets. While risks like trading volume volatility and rising expenses are worth monitoring and factoring into an investor's decision process, I'd be a buyer of the stock given the margin of safety on valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UiPath: Betting Big On Agentic AI, But Growth Seems Uncertain
data.description:
Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) seems to be embedded in everything we do these days, but the next stage of enthusiasm seems to be centred around Agentic AI, where software agents can independently act across complex tasks. Enterprise automation platform UiPath Inc. (NYSE:PATH) is one of the key players in this trend, but with plenty of uncertainty lingering in the market and wider economy, there are questions of whether the appetite for innovation outweighs the concerns I have with some fundamentals. I see plenty of reasons why this technology could have huge potential, but for now, I'm cautiously bearish, with recent results showing plenty of risks in execution, slowing demand, and genuine fragility in the sentiment of investors.

From RPA to Agentic AI

Most will know the company as a giant in the robotic process automation (RPA) niche, enabling companies to automate repetitive, rules-based tasks. However, with AI taking on a lot of this burden, the business has looked to pivot towards what it calls "Agentic automation", where AI can act independently, and address problems beyond a rules-based structure.

UiPath management spotlighted this transformation heavily in the latest Q4 presentation, with end-to-end orchestration, testing, and decision-making tools at the core of the offering going forward. With this platform in place, thousands of AI agents are in development via the agent builder, putting the company firmly on the map as a huge range of companies look to build a presence in the AI software market.

Slowing Growth As Economic Clouds Gather

UiPath's revenue in FY 2025 came in at $1.43 billion, up a decent 9% YoY, but this falls way behind the 30-40% which the market has become used to over recent years. ARR also rose 14% to $1.66 billion, representing decent growth in the subscription segment.

This is not a problem in itself, but with Cloud ARR growing much faster than the legacy business, and net retention somewhat plateauing, management is clearly having to work hard to keep existing customers, potentially reflecting the wider caution in spending across the enterprise IT landscape.

Most investors will understand the company is operating in a complex economic environment at present, with management specifically referring to geopolitical and economic issues in recent filings and earnings calls. With companies cutting down on enterprise software budgets and sales across Asia and Europe at a somewhat negative point in the cycle, the fight to meet expectations is intense for the leadership team.

Taking a closer look at the FY 2026 guidance shows that management is still in a fairly cautious place. Revenues look to be on the decline over the coming quarter, and with AI-driven technology setting some lofty ambitions, this is not likely to impress the investor community.

Still Unprofitable, But Making Progress

Most importantly, the business remains unprofitable. It saw a net loss of $163 million in FY 2025, slightly better than the year before. The chief culprit for now appears to be sales and marketing costs, at a hefty $739 million. While this plays a key role in maintaining customer retention and expansion, it is alongside a massive $381 million in R&D spend. Which of these levers management chooses to manage going forward will be critical as competition intensifies and as investors closely watch the progress towards profits.

I'm encouraged to see $1.9 billion in cash and equivalents, alongside no debt, giving management a decent amount of flexibility.

UiPath's stock has fallen a whopping 70% since IPO, but I'm still not convinced it's in buy territory just yet. At a P/S of 4.5 times, it still trades at a relative premium to many names in the RPA legacy space, but as expected, is below many of the software specialists it may be directly competing with going forward. My concern when putting it in this dynamic category is the lack of profitability and high expectations against firms with enormous resources, which management may struggle to meet.

Looking at the analyst community, many are setting fairly ambitious price targets, but with the average sitting around the $12 mark, this is marginally below the current level.

My own estimate of a fair price is a decent amount below this average price target, at $8. This is based on an 8% discount rate and 7% revenue growth, based on company guidance. This calculation leads to a present value of $4.5 billion and is based on 551.5 million shares outstanding.

Admittedly, there is a solid chance that AI features capture the attention of the market, sending shares higher amid a general resurgence, but with my estimate of fair value 35% below the current level, there feels like a long way down if the strategy disappoints the market.

Careful Steps Amid Fierce Competition

Obviously, UiPath is not alone in the AI automation arena. Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) is making huge moves to deploy automation tools in platforms, Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) is deploying AI across workstreams, and a plethora of others are building out capabilities. Alongside the big boys of the market, open-source alternatives and other international AI players have the potential to break into the market, severely impacting revenue potential.

Management is going to need to move carefully over the coming months to control the narrative and build out capabilities in a dynamic sector. I'm closely watching the following:

  1. Execution Risk - if products are delayed, or adoption is disappointing, growth estimates will be nearly impossible to meet.
  2. Macroeconomic Risk - ongoing tightening of budgets across enterprise firms will likely weaken ARR figures.
  3. Valuation Risk - if revenue guidance is not met, the market may further derate the stock.

Lots of Promise, But Equal Amounts of Uncertainty

The technology on offer here speaks for itself. AI agents could change the game across a huge range of sectors, but they currently sit at a crucial crossroads of promise and reality. UiPath has a strong balance sheet, sound plan, and capable management, but will need to show the market that revenue growth slowing down is just a blip.

Until Agentic AI really shows up in the numbers and in the earnings report, I'll cautiously avoid this one. I'd suggest that curious investors keep an eye on the ARR trends and uptake of these new features, but suggest that for now, the market is pricing in far more success than is currently on show in the fundamentals of the company.

data.title: Dividend Checkup Amid Trump Drug Pricing Order: I Prefer AbbVie Over Pfizer
data.description:
PFE, ABBV, and drug pricing executive order

I last wrote on AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV (NEOE:ABBV:CA) and Pfizer Inc. (NYSE:PFE) (NEOE:PFE:CA) both back in March 2024. My last ABBV article was titled “AbbVie: Margin Expansion Potential Can Support Elevated P/E,” and the PFE article was titled “Pfizer: Bulls Need To Check Out Its Graham P/E.” I rated both stocks as a hold at that time for the following considerations:

ABBV: I don’t see an obvious bias in AbbVie Inc.’s risk/return ratio. The company is grappling with diminishing Humira sales and is also trading at an above-average P/E. However, a potential profit margin expansion could support the current P/E.

PFE: Bulls have good reasons to love PFE, with an attractive dividend yield and a low P/E. But I urge potential investors to consider other contradicting factors. With its bleak growth outlook, the Graham P/E ratio suggests a valuation premium instead of a discount.

Since then, a few new catalysts have surfaced, both in terms of their specific businesses and also in terms of the macroscopic conditions facing the overall healthcare sector. Catalysts in the former category are largely reflected in the earnings reports (ERs) both companies have announced since my last writing, and this article will focus on their dividend payouts. This focus is chosen for a few reasons. First, for stocks like ABBV and PFE, which have a long track record of dividends, I consider their payout as a more reliable indicator of their growth potential.

The second reason is of more immediate relevance to the catalysts in the second category: the macroscopic parameters facing both stocks. On this front, I consider the tariff disputes that started in early April and the new Executive Order that President Trump just signed as the most important changes. The order aims to lower drug prices for American consumers by a substantial amount (in the range of 30% to 80%). At this point, I do not think there is sufficient information to assess the impacts of these variables on the profits for either ABBV or PFE, as a few counter-balancing forces could play out. For example, the net result will depend on how the specific pricing is determined in the U.S. vs. overseas.

Given these developments, I urge investors to focus on the return vectors that involve fewer uncertainties. And dividend payouts represent the vector with the least degree of ambiguity and uncertainty. Thus, the rest of this article will perform a holistic examination of ABBV and PFE’s dividends. In particular, it will involve a few aspects (such as buybacks and profit margins) that are often overlooked by dividend growth investors, as you can see from my following scorecard. In the end, my conclusion is that ABBV offers a much better package to weather the ongoing uncertainties than PFE. This conclusion therefore, leads me to maintain my Hold rating on ABBV while downgrading PFE to Sell.

1. ABBV vs. PFE stock: Dividend record

The remainder of this article will follow a comprehensive method that we’ve developed to select dividend stocks. The method is developed based on various insights gleaned from Peter Lynch’s writing. The method has been described in detail in our earlier articles. As such, this article will minimize the recap of the method and quote the most essential part as we go. The method, with no surprise, starts with establishing a long track record as described below:

Dividend Track Record: A long history of consistently increasing dividends is highly valued by Lynch. His guides are to look for companies with a track record of stable dividends for at least 20 years.

Both stocks easily satisfy the 20-year minimum requirements. In addition, ABBV is one of the S&P Dividend Aristocrats, an elite group of stocks that have increased their payouts consecutively for at least 25 years. Note that the table below shows that ABBV only has 11 years of dividend increases. This is because the table started counting only after ABBV’s spinoff from Abbott Laboratories in 2013. In contrast, PFE has been growing its dividend for 14 years so far.

As such, I consider ABBV’s dividend track record to be much stronger than PFE, and give it 1 point in the first round.

2. ABBV vs. PEF stock: valuation and PEGY ratios

The next step is to check the valuation. Since we are evaluating dividend stocks, the usual P/E ratio should be adjusted for dividend growth rates and also yield. The following PEGY ratio popularized by Peter Lynch provides a good approach for such adjustment.

PEG refers to the price-to-earnings growth ratio. He also introduced a modified version of the PEG ratio for dividends stocks. The modified ratio is called the PEGY ratio and is computed as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The concept is simple yet effective. If the dividends are high enough, investors should not expect (and do not need either) a high growth rate anymore. Just like the PEG ratio, Lynch favors PEGY ratios below 1x.

With this understanding, the next table summarizes my analysis of their PEGY ratios. Here, I plugged in A) ABBV and PFE’s dividend growth rate in the last 3 years tabulated above (i.e., 5.59% for ABBV and 2.47% for PFE), B) a FY1 P/E ratio of 15.35 for ABBV and 7.61 for PFE per consensus estimate shown in the next table below, and C) finally a FWD dividend yield of 3.49% for ABBV and 7.52% for PFE.

With these parameters, PFE’s PEGY ratio worked out to be 0.76x, translating into a substantial discount compared to the 1x ideal threshold. In contrast, ABBV’s PEGY ratio of 1.69x is significantly above the threshold.

As such, PFE clearly wins a point in this round.

3. ABBV vs. PFE: dividend safety

Dividend safety is a routine check for all dividend investors, so I will just go over this very briefly and only highlight a few caveats. Most investors start with payout ratios (i.e., dividend payout dividend EPS). However, EPS can fluctuate widely due to accounting codes. As a result, dividend payout ratios often provide no meaningful signal at all, which is exactly the case with ABBV and PFE, as you can see from the top panel of the following chart (top panel).

This is the reason I rely more on cash dividend payout ratios, as shown in the bottom panel. ABBV’s cash dividend payout ratio has been hovering around 60% in the past 2~3 years, and currently is 61.8% as seen. In contrast, PFE’s cash payout ratio exhibited high volatility. It exceeded 100% in 2024 and currently is 96.7%.

Thus, I consider ABBV as the winner in terms of safety.

4. ABBV vs. PFE stock: Share repurchases

Share repurchase is an area many investors overlook. However, they are equally valuable and important for the following reasons:

Share buybacks are just as valuable for total returns as cash dividends (even more so if you consider tax implications). Yet, it is overlooked by many dividend investors. Plus, share buybacks are a strong indicator of financial strength (that a company has excess cash flow beyond capital expenditures and dividend obligations) and also management's confidence in their own business.

On this front, ABBV has been a much more regular buyer of its own shares than PFE, as you can see from the next chart. To wit, ABBV has been consistently buying back its common shares in the past 5 years, with a net common buyback yield of around 0.5%. In contrast, PFE bought back some of its shares during 2022 and 2023, with a buyback yield of around 0.75% on average. But the repurchase has stopped since then.

As such, I also consider ABBV to be the winner in this round and assign it another point.

5. ABBV vs. PFE stock: profit margins

Finally, profitability metrics are also important in our model. They are not directly related to dividends. But better metrics can provide a thicker buffer against uncertainties, and thus lead to better dividend safety and stronger dividend growth potential. Under current conditions, we feel this is of particular relevance as both the evolving tariff rates and the new executive order create considerable earnings uncertainties for both ABBV and PFE.

Based on the profitability metrics provided in the chart below, I feel both PFE and ABBV are featuring robust profit measures. To start, both of them feature a very high gross margin. PFE has a Gross Profit Margin of 74.42%, and ABBV's reading is 71.01%. Walking down the income statement, the comparison varies moderately from metric to metric, as seen. For example, ABBV shows a greater EBIT Margin of 33.15% versus PFE's 26.33%, while PFE shows a higher net margin of 12.62% versus ABBV's 7.31%.

As such, I consider it a tie in this round and assign 1 point to each of them.

Other risks and final thoughts

To recap, the goal of this follow-up article is to reevaluate ABBV and PFE, given the latest policy changes that can impact their profits. From past experiences, I have learned that the more uncertain the situation is, the more I should focus on the return drivers that are less ambiguous. That is why this reevaluation examined both stocks through the window of dividend payouts and growth.

Based on this examination (with final results shown again here), we see ABBV as a more attractive candidate and thus repeat our hold rating. As for PFE, we see an overall weak package and thus downgrade our rating to sell. We feel this follow-up can be timely because many investors could be drawn to PFE’s unusually high yield while overlooking risks lurking beneath.

Finally, let me present a bigger picture to close out. The table below is a snapshot taken from our market dashboard. Our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) developed this tool to help us and our members obtain an overview of the market to better identify promising areas. It’s downloadable as a Google sheet. The health care sector, represented by XLV, where both ABBV and PFE are constituent holdings, appears very promising to us.

As seen, the healthcare sector is currently the 4th most attractively valued sector by dividend yield Z-score. We hope this bigger picture better contextualizes my above rating downgrade for PFE. As mouthwatering as its dividend yield is (around 7.5% as of this writing), we believe there are plenty of alternatives within the sector that offer a more favorable return/risk ratio. Besides ABBV, another alternative idea is JNJ, as detailed in our recent article.

data.title: Vinci Partners Is Taking Long To Execute, But I Keep My Buy Rating
data.description:
Vinci Partners Investments Ltd. (NASDAQ:VINP), previously Vinci Partners, reported Q1 2025 earnings, the second quarter after the merger of equals with Compass, and the first with complete three months of integration (versus two months in 4Q24).

The results are disappointing in some areas (overall margins, distribution) and ok in others (cost management, credit fundraising). Although I continue to take a long-term view and believe waiting for a year of operations with Compass is needed to judge the merger, I tend to believe the name is getting less attractive with fewer investment balances and lower profitability. For the time being, I maintain a Buy, but management has to deliver.

Analyzing the results and the context

Cost management: One of the key aspects to understand about the merger was the management of costs, to make the merger the most effective possible and raise the margins of the combined business.

To begin with, Vinci merged with a lower-margin business (Compass, mostly a third-party distribution business), and in addition, the integration could have costs.

In this respect, the situation was positive when comparing 1Q25 with 4Q24. Overall costs went up 20%, which is below what one would expect given the addition of a month of consolidation (33% of a quarter). In addition, the R$40 million non-recurring merger line from 4Q24 was completely eliminated in Q1, therefore making it truly non-recurring.

The majority of segments (except for Private Equity, managed by Vinci) saw similar margins between 4Q24 and 1Q25, indicating no worsening in the business.

The aggregate level of margins fell to 28.5% for fee-related earnings, below the guided low 30s. Management considered that this happened not because of costs but because of a lack of revenue in the advisory (a lumpy and non-recurring business), with BRL 0.5 million this quarter versus averages of about BRL 10 million per quarter.

Management said during the call that costs should grow only with inflation going forward, and I expect to check that they maintain that commitment. In a business with high operating leverage as investment management, controlling costs is paramount.

Mixed fundraising: One of the most important long-term drivers of the business is fundraising activity. The context in Brazil has been really hard for private equity strategies because interest rates have been very high. The recent Central Bank decisions to elevate the reference rate to 15% against an inflation of about 5/6% make this even harder.

Still, the company had a good quarter in credit. It raised BRL 200 million for SPS4 (special situation credit, matching the size of SPS3 in the first round), and another BRL 200 million for MAVIII (agri-credit). It also raised BRL 600 million for POCOII (Peru credit), the first indication of raising outside of Brazil. During the year, the private focus will be on credit: one from Latam Strategy and another from Colombia, both on the Compass side of the business, and another on Brazilian infrastructure, on the Vinci side.

With private strategies (equity, credit, and real assets) responsible for at least 2/3 of fee-related earnings, growing assets is important. The commitments of the quarter represent a growth of only 1% in AUM for the quarter. For the year, when adjusting for the effect of the merger with Compass, growth is only 6%. This is barely above BRL inflation, and therefore too low.

It makes sense that credit strategies are more favored in the current higher-for-longer rates context. Hopefully, the company can improve fundraising going forward.

Distribution also mixed: The largest part of the Compass business is concentrated in the Global IP&S segment, which mainly distributes Liquid (open-end) and Alternative (closed-end) funds. The company does not provide the figures for each side of third-party distribution (TPD).

This complicates understanding the situation of the business. On the one hand, it suffered net outflows for BRL 7 billion (3% for the quarter), but management said that most came from lower-earning liquid funds, and mostly related to global volatility, whereas the alternatives distribution received inflows. Unfortunately, the exact figures were not provided.

The margins of the Global IP&S business were flat on 1Q25 vs 4Q24, implying therefore no deterioration of the business overall, otherwise operating leverage would have been negative. Still, if the business continues to deteriorate, it will be a negative more against the merger with Compass.

Vinci over the years

I have been recommending Vinci for almost three years, during which the stock price has barely moved, but it has paid dividends. The return is commensurate with the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ).

Over those years, the business has grown mainly via acquisitions of other businesses, the last of which is Compass. Each of these consumed cash and investments that the company had conserved from its IPO in 2021. The company has also bought back shares in the process (and continues to do so at a rate of about 600 thousand, 1% of the share count per quarter).

Fee-related earnings per share, a good measure of the recurring business, have grown almost 30% in the past three years, or about BRL 1 per year. However, the cost in terms of net investments per share has been almost BRL 12, not a particularly good interchange.

The context has not been super good. Brazil has grown between 2022 and 2025, but the country has also dealt with a very high interest rate, which is not conducive to alternative investments.

Still, I tend to have increased doubts about the execution ability of Vinci's management. The merger with Compass added to this, because Compass is a distribution business, rather than an investment manager, and the synergies between Brazil and other Latin American countries are not clear.

The positives for the business, particularly if Brazil enjoys a period of higher foreign investment, lower interest rates, and higher currency appreciation, are many. We could say that the current earnings are representative of a negative period, and that the average of a cycle is probably a little higher.

Valuation

Assuming the company can meet its soft guidance of a low 30s fee-related earnings margin, on revenues of about BRL 1 billion, it could generate close to BRL 300 million in fee-related earnings. Adding another BRL 20 million in performance earnings (more lumpy, but averaging several quarters), we get BRL 320 million.

From this figure, we have to remove about BRL 20 million in share-based compensation. We ignore the (net positive) effect of GP investment income plus interest income minus interest expenses because we will be removing the net investments from the cost of each share. From the resulting BRL 300 million, we remove 18% in taxes (commented as the potential tax rate in the 4Q24 call) and reach net earnings of about BRL 250 million, or about $45 million.

Compared to this, the company trades at a market cap of $650 million and owns about BRL 800 million ($140 million) in net investments. The result is a net multiple on earnings of bout 11x.

In my opinion, this is not a super high multiple, but it is not cheap for a Brazilian financial company, either. Below are the forward multiples of many other Brazilian financial companies.

On the positive side, the earnings (and conversely the multiple) represent a depressed period for alternative managers in Brazil and, more generally, in Latin America. On the other hand, I do not have so much confidence in Vinci's management anymore.

For the time being, considering that the multiple is not excessive, and that the merger has had less than a year to proceed, I prefer to continue maintaining a Buy rating on Vinci. Still, I expect the company to provide measures of execution this year in order to keep the rating.

data.title: CAE: A Gem To Capitalize On Commercial Aviation And Defense Demand
data.description:
CAE (NYSE:CAE) is one of the companies that, I believe, can capitalize on the increasing demand for training services and hardware in the aerospace and defense industries. Since my last report on CAE, the stock price did increase by less than a percent, while the market is down 4%.

This stock price return doesn’t quite match my expectations for names that I have a buy rating on, but can be explained by the global trade turmoil we have seen. CAE is a Canadian company, and the trade war with Canada introduced uncertainty on US market access. Currently, it seems that aerospace parts and products are exempt from the tariffs, as discussed by the CEO of Bombardier (OTCQX:BDRAF).

In this report, I discuss the most recent earnings and I update my stock price target for this name that remains buy rated.

Dollar figures in this report are Canadian dollars, unless mentioned otherwise.

CAE Adjusted Income Rises Faster Than Revenues

Full-year revenues increased 10% to $4.7 billion, while cost of sales increased 9%, driving a gross margin expansion from 27% to 27.6%. Operating income increased from a $185.4 million loss to $729.2 million, driven by the absence of the impairment of goodwill to re-baseline its defense business and lower restructuring costs in FY24. On an adjusted basis, the profit increased 33% with margins of 15.6% compared to 12.9%. So, we see that costs relative to revenues have declined. Sales to the US accounted for 47.6% of all sales. CAE has a subsidiary in the US, but realistically, tariffs are not good for its business, even if major aerospace parts and components are exempt.

Revenues for Civil Aviation rose 11% to $2.7 billion accounting for 57.5% of the sales while the remainder was driven by Defense and Security sales where growth was 8%. Civil Aviation gross margins only grew 2% as a less favorable sales mix affected the margins with lower contributions from commercial aviation training and airline digital solutions. This resulted in adjusted income growth of 6%, well below the double-digit sales growth.

The defense segment was somewhat more exciting in terms of improvement in the results. Gross margins grew from 15.5% to 20.8%, driven by the absence of unfavorable profit adjustments. Adjusted segment earnings rose from break-even levels to $150.5 million, driven by the same absence of adjustments on what the company calls Legacy Contracts.

A Look At The Market Dynamics

The adjusted backlog grew sharply from $12.1 billion to $20.1 billion. Civil Aviation backlog grew from $6.4 billion to $8.85 billion, as the higher stake the company has in SIMCOM requires the backlog to be added to the backlog. For the full year, Civil Aviation posted a 1.4x book-to-bill ratio, which signals healthy demand. Defense backlog increased sharply from $5.7 billion to $11.3 billion. This was driven by a higher joint venture backlog following the $4.7 billion Future Aircrew Training contract award from Canada.

The Civil Aviation is seeing robust demand from Asia, partially offset by pilot activity in the US, but the conditions are improving, and I believe that an increase in new airplane deliveries that should materialize this year will drive pilot training demand. The company also expanded to air traffic control training, which given the ATC conditions in the US may provide a strong driver of growth.

In the defense business, the company can benefit from the Rearm program in Europe as well as expanding defense budgets elsewhere. The company is structuring the business such that it has a part of the business focusing on Canada and the US and a second part of the business focusing on other international sales.

For the full year, there are growth drivers for sales and higher profits, supported by strong visibility in the backlog and market. The company also targets a 150% free cash flow conversion.

Not unimportant in the current global context are comments on tariffs which CAE provided during the Q4 2025 earnings call:

CAE is well insulated from direct tariff impacts. Approximately 70% of our CAE's total revenues come from services delivered within our customers' own countries, which significantly limits our exposure to cross-border tariffs. For the US market, this proportion was even higher, around 80% last year. Moreover, our flagship product, the Full Flight Simulator, is exempt from tariffs under the USMCA. From an enterprise risk standpoint, with roughly one-third of our workforce based in the United States, a substantial operating footprint, and an already significant US bill of materials, we're confident that we have the flexibility to effectively manage any residual risk exposure.

So what we see is that after an initial scare for many Canadian Aerospace and Defense companies, the realization has settled that its products are largely exempt from tariffs and CAE has some protection in the sense that its business does deliver hardware, but a significant portion of revenues comes from services. Furthermore, the full motion flight simulators are exempt from the tariffs under USMCA.

CAE Stock Has Significant Upside Ahead

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

CAE definitely did not have it easy the past few years, as air travel demand had to rebound, and airplane production had to increase to support higher demand for training services. Beyond that, inflation provided a challenging backdrop for its defense work. However, the company is now able to look at a future that is starting to improve. EBITDA is set to grow by 8.4% annually through 2027, and free cash flow growth has been modelled along the same rates.

The company is currently undervalued by around 21%, and its forward earnings would even indicate a 41% upside with a $34 price target in USD. That is also where I believe the CAE stock should be trading.

Conclusion: CAE Stock Has Upside On Revitalizing End Markets

I believe that CAE is positioned well to benefit from demand from defense training services and an uptick in commercial training services and hardware in the second half of the year. The company can lean on attractive demand drivers, and it is working its way on closing its legacy defense contracts that have been a drag on margins, and with each contract closing, it can replace those challenged programs with high-margin work, providing a better margin mix.

At the time of writing, CAE stock is down 5.9%, but I do believe there is enough reason to be positive about the company’s prospects and reiterate my buy rating.
